analogy: Think of "Laws of Motion" through the chapter's own opening images: CHAPTER FIVE WORK, ENERGY AND POWER 5.1 INTRODUCTION The terms ‘work’, ‘energy’ and ‘power’ are frequently used in everyday language. A farmer ploughing the field, a construction worker carrying bricks, a student studying for a competitive examination, an artist painting a beautiful landscape, all are... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER FIVE, WORK, ENERGY AND POWER, 5.1 INTRODUCTION, 5.1.1 The Scalar Product. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Laws of Motion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHAPTER FIVE WORK, ENERGY AND POWER 5.1 INTRODUCTION The terms ‘work’, ‘energy’ and ‘power’ are frequently used in everyday language.

The text explains that a farmer ploughing the field, a construction worker carrying bricks, a student studying for a competitive examination, an artist painting a beautiful landscape, all are said to be working.

The text explains that in physics, however, the word ‘Work’ covers a definite and precise meaning.

The text explains that somebody who has the capacity to work for 14-16 hours a day is said to have a large stamina or energy.

The text explains that we admire a long distance runner for her stamina or energy.

The text explains that in Physics too, the term ‘energy’ is related to work in this sense, but as said above the term ‘work’ itself is defined much more precisely.

The text explains that the word ‘power’ is used in everyday life with different shades of meaning.

The text explains that in karate or boxing we talk of ‘powerful’ punches.

The text explains that this shade of meaning is close to the meaning of the word ‘power’ used in physics.

The text explains that we shall find that there is at best a loose correlation between the physical definitions and the physiological pictures these terms generate in our minds.

The text explains that the aim of this chapter is to develop an understanding of these three physical quantities.

The text explains that before we proceed to this task, we need to develop a mathematical prerequisite, namely the scalar product of two vectors.

The text explains that 5.1.1 The Scalar Product We have learnt about vectors and their use in Chapter 3.

The text explains that physical quantities like displacement, velocity, acceleration, force etc. are vectors.

The text explains that we have also learnt how vectors are added or subtracted.

The text explains that we now need to know how vectors are multiplied.

The text explains that we shall look at the vector product in Chapter 6.

The text explains that each vector, A and B, has a direction but their scalar product does not have a direction.

The text explains that the object undergoes a displacement d in the positive x-direction as shown in Fig.

The text explains that 5.2 NOTIONS OF WORK AND KINETIC ENERGY: THE WORK-ENERGY THEOREM The following relation for rectilinear motion under constant acceleration a has been encountered in Chapter 3, v2 − u2 = 2 as (5.2) where u and v are the initial and final speeds and s the distance traversed.

The text explains that the right side is a product of the displacement and the component of the force along the displacement.

The text explains that during this process, the force on the cycle due to the road is 200 N and is directly opposed to the motion.

The text explains that the kinetic energy of an object is a measure of the work an The work done by the force is defined to be the product of component of the force in the direction of the displacement and the magnitude of this displacement.

The text explains that for the block moving on a smooth horizontal table, the gravitational force mg does no work since it acts at right angles to the displacement.

The text explains that if we assume that the moon’s orbit around the earth is perfectly circular then the earth’s gravitational force does no work.

The text explains that the moon’s instantaneous displacement is tangential while the earth’s force is radially inwards and q = p/2.

The text explains that it opposes motion and acts in a direction opposite to F.

The text explains that in the scalar form, information with respect to directions contained in Newton’s second law is not present.

The text explains that the earth’s crust is not uniform, but has discontinuities and dislocations that are called fault lines.

The text explains that these fault lines in the earth’s crust are like ‘compressed springs’.

The text explains that an earthquake results when these fault lines readjust.

The text explains that the gravitational force on a ball of mass m is mg . g may be treated as a constant near the earth surface.

The text explains that by ‘near’ we imply that the height h of the ball above the earth’s surface is very small compared to the earth’s radius RE (h <<RE) so that we can ignore the variation of g near the earth’s surface*.

The text explains that in what follows we have taken the upward direction to be positive.

The text explains that mathematically, (for simplicity, in one dimension) the potential * The variation of g with height is discussed in Chapter 7 on Gravitation.

The text explains that in the previous chapter we have worked on examples dealing with inclined planes.

The text explains that over the whole path, xi to xf, this means that Ki + V(xi ) = Kf + V(xf ) (5.11) The quantity K +V(x), is called the total mechanical energy of the system.

The text explains that thus, the principle of conservation of total mechanical energy can be stated as The total mechanical energy of a system is conserved if the forces, doing work on it, are conservative.

The text explains that the total mechanical energy E of the system is conserved.

The text explains that we take the potential energy of the system to be zero at the lowest point A.

The text explains that if the connecting string is cut at this instant, the bob will execute a projectile motion with horizontal projection akin to a rock kicked horizontally from the edge of a cliff.

The text explains that specifically, if the block is pulled from xi and allowed to return to xi ; W k x x k x k x s i i x x i i = − = − ∫ d 2 2 2 2 = 0 (5.18) The work done by the spring force in a cyclic process is zero.

The text explains that we define the potential energy V(x) of the spring to be zero when block and spring system is in the equilibrium position.

The text explains that a solution of Newton’s Second Law for this system is required for temporal information.

The text explains that for the constant gravitational force mg, we took V = 0 on the earth’s surface.

The text explains that in a later chapter we shall see that for the force due to the universal law of gravitation, the zero is best defined at an infinite distance from the gravitational source.

The text explains that at the same time, we try to discover physical quantities, which do not change in a physical process.

The text explains that the masses m1 and m2 fly-off in different directions.

The text explains that pHYSICS 84 5.11.1 Elastic and Inelastic Collisions In all collisions the total linear momentum is conserved; the initial momentum of the system is equal to the final momentum of the system.

The text explains that 5.21 The blades of a windmill sweep out a circle of area A.

The text explains that (a) If the wind flows at a velocity v perpendicular to the circle, what is the mass of the air passing through it in time t ?

The text explains that (c) Assume that the windmill converts 25% of the wind’s energy into electrical energy, and that A = 30 m2, v = 36 km/h and the density of air is 1.2 kg m–3.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Physics, Chapter 5: Laws of Motion

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
